pattern: Maintaining a sorted set of merged intervals lets each new number be absorbed by locating its neighbors and coalescing only the touching ranges, keeping the interval list always compact and ordered.
hints: Storing every number individually wastes space; what the query wants is the set of maximal runs of consecutive integers.
When a new number arrives it can extend an interval on its left, extend one on its right, bridge a gap between two, or start a fresh one.

Keep the intervals sorted by start and, on each insert, find where the number lands; merge with an adjacent interval whose end is one less, or one whose start is one more, joining both if it sits exactly between them.

edge_inputs.description: the statement's own sequence: scattered inserts that later bridge into bigger runs
a single number is an interval of length one
adding a number already covered must not change the intervals
a number exactly between two intervals fuses all three into one
consecutive ascending inserts grow a single interval

stress.description: many random inserts over a small range so merges and bridges happen often, with frequent getIntervals checks
stress.note: small value space forces dense coalescing; getIntervals output is the canonical sorted interval list

complexity.time: O(n) per insert worst case, O(k) per query
